meta.title: MS-PBI-2: Modeling, DAX, Visuals and Governance

questions.stem: You use a deployment pipeline (Dev → Test → Production) in Power BI. After deploying to Production, you discover that dataset connection strings still point to the Test database. Which action should you configure to fix this for future deployments?

options.text: Configure deployment pipeline rules to map parameters or data source connection details between stages
options.explanation: Power BI deployment pipelines support deployment rules that automatically transform dataset properties (data source connection strings, Power Query parameter values) when deploying between stages. Rules are configured per pipeline stage and apply during each deployment, eliminating manual post-deployment updates.

options.text: Use Power Query parameters set to the Test environment values in all stages
options.explanation: Parameters set to Test environment values would still point to Test after deployment unless a rule remaps them to Production values.

options.text: Create separate PBIX files for each environment
options.explanation: Maintaining separate PBIX files per environment creates duplication and makes version management difficult; deployment pipeline rules are the purpose-built solution.

options.text: Manually update connection strings after each deployment in Production
options.explanation: Manually updating connection strings after each deployment is error-prone and does not scale.

questions.reference: [Create deployment rules](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/cicd/deployment-pipelines/create-rules)

questions.stem: You want report consumers to be able to drill through from a summary sales page to a detailed transactions page filtered by the selected product. How do you configure this?

options.text: Use the Sync slicers feature to synchronize the product slicer across pages
options.explanation: Sync slicers keeps slicer selections in sync across pages but does not enable right-click drill-through navigation.

options.text: Add the Product field to the Drill through well of the detail page in the Visualizations pane
options.explanation: Power BI drill-through is configured on the destination page by adding the field to the Drill through well in the Visualizations pane. When a user right-clicks a visual on the source page and selects 'Drill through > [detail page name]', Power BI navigates to the detail page with the selected field value as the filter context.

options.text: Enable Page Navigation in the summary visual's Format pane
options.explanation: Page Navigation buttons navigate to pages unconditionally; they do not pass the selected product as a filter context.

options.text: Create a bookmark for the detail page and link it from the summary visual
options.explanation: Bookmarks capture a static report state; they do not dynamically filter based on the user's selected product.

questions.reference: [Drillthrough in Power BI Reports: Navigate to Detailed Insights - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/create-reports/desktop-drillthrough)

questions.stem: A report uses a measure [Profit Margin %] = DIVIDE([Profit], [Revenue]). When added to a matrix with subtotals, the subtotal rows show incorrect margin percentages (they appear to be averaging the row percentages). What is the root cause?

options.text: The measure is working correctly; the user misunderstands margin aggregation
options.explanation: While DAX does re-evaluate measures in subtotal context, the symptom described (appearing to average row percentages) indicates the underlying measures are aggregating incorrectly — not that the user is wrong.

options.text: Use SUMX over the matrix rows to produce correct subtotals
options.explanation: SUMX iterates rows and would not produce the correct subtotal margin either; the issue is with how the underlying Profit and Revenue measures aggregate.

options.text: DIVIDE averages its inputs at aggregation levels by default; use SUM instead
options.explanation: DIVIDE does not average its inputs; it divides them. If the result looks like an average of child rows, the numerator or denominator measures are aggregating incorrectly.

options.text: Matrix subtotals apply SUM aggregation to the measure values of child rows instead of re-evaluating the measure in the subtotal's filter context
options.explanation: When a matrix shows subtotals, Power BI re-evaluates the DAX measure at the subtotal's filter context (the combined filter of the subtotal group). If subtotals look wrong, the issue is typically that [Profit] or [Revenue] measures have incorrect aggregation at the subtotal level (e.g., using AVERAGE instead of SUM), causing the ratio to appear as an average of child row percentages rather than the correctly computed ratio.

questions.reference: [Avoid using FILTER as a filter argument in DAX - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/guidance/dax-avoid-avoid-filter-as-filter-argument)

questions.stem: You must show the percentage contribution of each product to total sales in a bar chart, where the total always represents 100% of currently filtered data. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Use DIVIDE([Product Sales], SUM(Sales[Amount]))
options.explanation: DIVIDE([Product Sales], SUM(Sales[Amount])) in a per-product context returns 100% for every row since SUM(Sales[Amount]) equals [Product Sales] at the product granularity.

options.text: Use DIVIDE([Product Sales], CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALLSELECTED(Products)))
options.explanation: ALLSELECTED(Products) removes filters from the Products table while preserving any external slicer/filter context. This means the denominator reflects the total sales of all currently visible products (respecting slicers outside the visual) rather than all products in the dataset.

options.text: Use DIVIDE([Product Sales], CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALL(Products)))
options.explanation: ALL(Products) always sums all products regardless of slicers, so the denominator does not change when users filter by region or category.

options.text: Change the bar chart Y-axis to percentage format
options.explanation: Changing the Y-axis to percentage format only changes the display format of the existing measure values; it does not compute a share-of-total.

questions.reference: [ALLSELECTED function (DAX) - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/allselected-function-dax)

questions.stem: A report page has a bar chart and a table visual. You want to configure the bar chart so that clicking a bar filters the table, but the table does not filter the bar chart when a row is selected. How do you configure this?

options.text: Select the table, go to Format > Edit Interactions, and set the bar chart interaction to None
options.explanation: This only configures the table-to-bar-chart direction; it does not ensure the bar chart filters the table.

options.text: Select the bar chart, go to Format > Edit Interactions, set table to Filter; then select the table and set the bar chart to None
options.explanation: Edit Interactions is set FROM the source visual TO each target. To make the bar chart filter the table: select bar chart, set table to Filter (default). To prevent the table from filtering the bar chart: select table, set bar chart to None. Both interactions need to be configured independently.

options.text: Disable cross-filtering in the report settings
options.explanation: Disabling cross-filtering globally would prevent the bar chart from filtering the table as well, which is the opposite of the desired behavior.

options.text: Select the bar chart, go to Format > Edit Interactions, and set the table interaction to Filter
options.explanation: This only configures one direction (bar chart filters table) but does not prevent the table from also filtering the bar chart.

questions.reference: [Change How Visuals Interact in a Report - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/create-reports/service-reports-visual-interactions)

questions.stem: In a Power BI data model, the relationship between a Dates table and a Sales fact table is a one-to-many relationship with a single-directional cross-filter. An analyst reports that a slicer on the Dates table does not filter a separate Products dimension table. Which action should you do?

options.text: Change the Dates-to-Sales relationship to bidirectional cross-filter
options.explanation: Enabling bidirectional on Dates-to-Sales would allow filters to flow from Sales back to Dates, which risks incorrect aggregations and ambiguous filter paths in the model.

options.text: Create a direct relationship between the Dates table and the Products table
options.explanation: Creating a direct Dates-to-Products relationship would introduce a many-to-many relationship or ambiguous filter path and is not the recommended approach.

options.text: Add Products as a role-playing dimension connected to Sales
options.explanation: A role-playing dimension is a technique for having a table serve multiple relationship roles (e.g., Order Date and Ship Date); it does not solve the cross-filter propagation issue.

options.text: Enable bidirectional cross-filter on the Sales-to-Products relationship
options.explanation: In a standard star schema, filters propagate from dimension tables to fact tables (one-to-many, single direction). For a Dates slicer to filter Products, the filter must travel: Dates → Sales → Products. This requires the Sales-to-Products relationship to allow bidirectional cross-filter, or you use CROSSFILTER in DAX.

questions.reference: [Bi-directional relationship guidance - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/guidance/relationships-bidirectional-filtering)

questions.stem: You have a many-to-many relationship between Products and Promotions (a product can have multiple promotions; a promotion can apply to multiple products). How should you model this in Power BI?

options.text: Use a calculated table with CROSSJOIN to generate all combinations
options.explanation: CROSSJOIN generates all possible combinations, creating a much larger table with spurious rows that do not represent actual product-promotion assignments.

options.text: Create a direct many-to-many relationship between Products and Promotions tables
options.explanation: While Power BI supports native many-to-many relationships (set cardinality to Many-to-Many), this approach is less explicit and can produce unexpected aggregation behavior without a bridge table.

options.text: Duplicate the Promotions table and create two one-to-many relationships
options.explanation: Duplicating the Promotions table creates data maintenance issues and does not correctly represent the many-to-many structure.

options.text: Create a bridge table ProductPromotions with ProductID and PromotionID, create one-to-many relationships from Products and Promotions to the bridge table
options.explanation: A bridge (junction) table with one row per product-promotion pair creates a proper schema. One-to-many relationships from Products → ProductPromotions and Promotions → ProductPromotions allow correct aggregation with explicit control over semantics.

questions.reference: [Many-to-many relationship guidance - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/guidance/relationships-many-to-many)

questions.stem: A measure returns different results when placed in a table visual versus a card visual. In the table it shows per-product values correctly; in the card (no row context) it returns blank. The measure is: Sales Rank = RANKX(ALL(Products[ProductName]), [Total Sales]). What is the issue?

options.text: The card visual does not support rank measures; use a table visual instead
options.explanation: Card visuals do support rank measures; the issue is semantic, not a visual limitation.

options.text: In the card visual there is no row filter context, so RANKX ranks all products and returns 1 for the grand total — the measure is working correctly
options.explanation: While RANKX returning 1 at the grand total level is technically how the function evaluates, the stated problem is that the card shows a misleading value. Adding ISINSCOPE is the fix, not accepting the behavior.

options.text: Add ISINSCOPE(Products[ProductName]) to return blank at the grand total level intentionally
options.explanation: RANKX over ALL(Products[ProductName]) with no row context (card visual) evaluates [Total Sales] for every product and ranks the grand total among them — typically returning 1. If the intent is to show rank only at the product level, wrapping with IF(ISINSCOPE(Products[ProductName]), RANKX(...), BLANK()) suppresses the misleading grand total rank. This is the standard rank measure pattern.

options.text: RANKX requires an explicit column in the first argument; use ALLSELECTED instead of ALL
options.explanation: ALLSELECTED would respect external slicers, which changes the ranking scope but does not fix the grand total issue on the card visual.

questions.reference: [RANKX function (DAX) - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/rankx-function-dax)

questions.stem: A workspace contains reports used by 500 users. You must ensure the workspace can handle concurrent query load without throttling. Which Power BI license/capacity model is required?

options.text: Power BI Premium Per User (PPU) licenses for all 500 users
options.explanation: Premium Per User (PPU) provides Premium features per user but runs on shared infrastructure, not dedicated capacity. For 500 concurrent users, shared infrastructure may still throttle.

options.text: Power BI Premium Per Capacity (P-SKU) assigned to the workspace
options.explanation: Power BI Premium Per Capacity (P-SKU) assigns dedicated compute capacity to a workspace, providing isolated resources that scale with the SKU size. This handles high concurrent query loads without throttling shared Pro capacity resources.

options.text: Power BI Embedded (A-SKU) for API-based distribution
options.explanation: Power BI Embedded (A-SKU) is designed for embedding reports in custom applications via API, not for standard Power BI service access for 500 users.

options.text: 500 individual Power BI Pro licenses
options.explanation: Power BI Pro licenses provide access to the shared Power BI service capacity, which can throttle under high concurrent load.

questions.reference: [What is Power BI Premium? - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/enterprise/service-premium-what-is)

questions.stem: A report page has a slicer for [Year] and a line chart showing monthly sales. You want the chart to show all years' data as a comparison regardless of the Year slicer selection. Which action should you do?

options.text: Set the visual-level filter on the line chart to exclude the Year field
options.explanation: Setting a visual-level filter to exclude Year would hide all data filtered by Year but would not allow all years to appear simultaneously.

options.text: Create a measure using ALL(Dates[Year]) to remove the year filter context
options.explanation: Creating a measure with ALL(Dates[Year]) removes the Year filter from the measure's computation, but other visuals on the page would still be filtered by the Year slicer.

options.text: Duplicate the page and remove the slicer from the duplicate
options.explanation: Duplicating the page and removing the slicer is a workaround that creates maintenance overhead; Edit Interactions is the correct purpose-built approach.

options.text: Use Edit interactions from the slicer to set the interaction with the line chart to 'None'
options.explanation: Power BI's Edit Interactions feature allows you to control whether a slicer or visual filters another visual. Setting the interaction from the Year slicer to the line chart to 'None' (the crossed-out icon) prevents the slicer from filtering that visual. Edit Interactions is accessed by selecting the slicer and clicking Format > Edit interactions.

questions.reference: [Change How Visuals Interact in a Report - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/create-reports/service-reports-visual-interactions)

questions.stem: You must prevent report authors from connecting to a dataset in a workspace unless their workspace is in the same Premium capacity. Which setting enforces this?

options.text: Remove Build permission from all users for the dataset
options.explanation: Removing Build permission entirely would prevent all external connections including legitimate ones from users within the same Premium capacity.

options.text: Enable 'Block republish and disable package refresh' in workspace settings
options.explanation: 'Block republish and disable package refresh' controls workspace-level publishing behavior, not cross-workspace live connections to datasets.

options.text: Enable XMLA endpoint restriction to Premium-only connections
options.explanation: XMLA endpoint restriction controls third-party tool access (e.g., Excel, Tabular Editor) to the dataset, not Power BI report live connections.

options.text: Restrict cross-workspace access using 'Discourage live connections from reports on other workspaces' in dataset settings
options.explanation: Power BI dataset settings include 'Discourage live connections from reports on other workspaces', which prevents report authors in other workspaces from creating live connections to the dataset. This enforces colocation of reports and datasets.

questions.reference: [Control the use of semantic models across workspaces - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/connect-data/service-datasets-admin-across-workspaces)

questions.stem: You must add a column in Power Query that extracts the domain name from an email address column (e.g., 'user@company.com' → 'company.com'). Which M expression is most readable and purpose-built for this transformation?

options.text: Text.Middle([Email], Text.PositionOf([Email], "@") + 1)
options.explanation: Text.Middle with Text.PositionOf works but requires calculating the position manually, making it more verbose than necessary.

options.text: Text.Split([Email], "@"){1}
options.explanation: Text.Split splits the string into a list and {1} retrieves the second element (the domain). This works but is less readable than Text.AfterDelimiter and requires knowledge of list indexing.

options.text: Text.AfterDelimiter([Email], "@")
options.explanation: Text.AfterDelimiter is the Power Query function specifically designed to extract text after a delimiter character. It is the most readable and semantically clear approach for this transformation, and it handles edge cases like multiple '@' symbols by defaulting to the first occurrence.

options.text: Text.Select([Email], {"a".."z", "."})
options.explanation: Text.Select filters characters from a set, which would remove the '@' but would also remove digits and hyphens that are valid in domain names (e.g., 'company-1.com' would lose the '1').

questions.reference: [Text.AfterDelimiter - PowerQuery M](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powerquery-m/text-afterdelimiter)

questions.stem: A large Power BI dataset (import mode, 20 GB) refreshes daily and the full refresh takes 4 hours. Business users need data refreshed every 30 minutes for recent transactions. Which strategy reduces refresh time while keeping historical data current?

options.text: Switch the entire dataset to DirectQuery
options.explanation: Switching to DirectQuery for a 20 GB dataset would create serious query performance problems for interactive dashboard users.

options.text: Enable incremental refresh with a 'detect data changes' column to refresh only the current day's partition and keep historical partitions frozen
options.explanation: Power BI incremental refresh splits the dataset into date partitions. Historical partitions are frozen after the incremental period and are never re-refreshed. Only the current partition (e.g., today) is refreshed on each cycle, reducing refresh time from hours to minutes. The 'detect data changes' option uses a watermark column to skip refreshes if source data has not changed.

options.text: Split into two datasets: historical (daily refresh) and current-day (DirectQuery)
options.explanation: Splitting into two datasets doubles data management complexity and creates challenges for analyses that span historical and recent data.

options.text: Reduce the dataset size by removing historical data older than 1 year
options.explanation: Removing historical data destroys valuable trend history and does not solve the need for frequent refresh of recent transactions.

questions.reference: [Configure incremental refresh and real-time data for Power BI semantic models - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/connect-data/incremental-refresh-overview)

questions.stem: You create a relationship between FactSales[ProductID] (many side) and DimProduct[ProductID] (one side). The relationship is valid in Power BI. What must be true about DimProduct[ProductID]?

options.text: It must match every ProductID value found in FactSales
options.explanation: Referential integrity (every FactSales ProductID must exist in DimProduct) is not enforced in Power BI relationships; mismatched keys result in null values for the dimension attributes, not an error.

options.text: It must be set as the table's key column in the model view
options.explanation: Power BI does not require you to explicitly designate a key column in the model view; uniqueness is what matters.

options.text: It must be an integer data type
options.explanation: The data type does not need to be integer; any comparable type (string, integer, GUID) works as long as the values match between the two tables.

options.text: It must be the primary key of DimProduct with no duplicate or blank values
options.explanation: The 'one' side of a one-to-many relationship in Power BI must have unique, non-null values. Power BI enforces this constraint — if DimProduct[ProductID] contains duplicates or blanks, the relationship cannot be created as a 1:many relationship (it would become a many:many).

questions.reference: [Model relationships in Power BI Desktop - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/transform-model/desktop-relationships-understand)

questions.stem: You connect to a SQL Server database in Power Query. The query retrieves 2 million rows but only 100,000 are needed after filtering. You want to reduce data transfer and improve refresh performance. Which action should you do?

options.text: Use DirectQuery mode instead of Import so no data is transferred
options.explanation: DirectQuery queries the source on every visual interaction but does not reduce data volume; it shifts when the query runs, not how much data is processed.

options.text: Apply a filter step in Power Query after importing all rows, then fold the query back to SQL
options.explanation: Applying a filter after importing all rows brings all 2 million rows into Power Query before filtering; 'folding back' is not how Power Query works — you need the filter before non-foldable steps.

options.text: Import all rows and use a calculated column with IF to exclude unnecessary rows
options.explanation: Using a calculated column with IF to mark rows does not reduce the data loaded; all 2 million rows are still imported and stored in the model.

options.text: Enable query folding by applying the filter step immediately after the source step so the filter is pushed to SQL Server
options.explanation: Query folding translates Power Query transformation steps into native SQL query operations executed on the source database. Applying the filter step directly after the Source step maximizes the chance of folding, pushing the WHERE clause to SQL Server and reducing data transfer.

questions.reference: [Understanding Query Evaluation and Query Folding in Power Query - Power Query](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-query/power-query-folding)

questions.stem: A Power Query refresh fails with 'Formula.Firewall: Query references other queries or steps and so may not directly access a data source.' What is the cause and fix?

options.text: The model has circular references between queries; remove the circular dependency
options.explanation: Circular references produce a different error; the Formula Firewall error is specifically about privacy levels.

options.text: The Power Query engine cannot process more than one data source per query
options.explanation: Power Query can process multiple data sources in a single query; combining sources is the common use case that triggers the privacy check.

options.text: The query references a step in a different query; merge the steps into a single query
options.explanation: Merging steps into a single query would not resolve the privacy-level conflict; the fix is to configure privacy settings.

options.text: Privacy levels are not set; two data sources with different privacy levels are being combined without explicit permission
options.explanation: The Formula Firewall prevents Power Query from combining data from sources with different privacy levels (Public, Organizational, Private) unless explicitly configured. This protects against data leakage. The fix is to set privacy levels on data sources in File > Options > Privacy, or in the on-premises data gateway settings.

questions.reference: [Behind the scenes of the Data Privacy Firewall - Power Query](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-query/data-privacy-firewall)

questions.stem: A measure [Sales LY] uses CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Dates[Date])). When the report is filtered to a single month (March 2024), [Sales LY] returns March 2023 correctly. But when filtered to a custom date range (Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2024), [Sales LY] returns an incorrect value. Why?

options.text: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR requires a contiguous date range; partial month ranges are not supported
options.explanation: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR works with any set of dates, including partial months and custom ranges; it does not require a contiguous or full-period range.

options.text: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR only works with standard calendar periods (month, quarter, year); use DATEADD(Dates[Date], -1, YEAR) for custom ranges
options.explanation: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR works on any set of dates, not only standard calendar periods. DATEADD is an alternative but SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR is not broken here.

options.text: The Dates table is missing a row for Feb 15, 2023, causing the calculation to return null
options.explanation: A missing date row would cause that specific date to return null, not the entire range; also, the Dates table should contain all dates in the model range.

options.text: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR shifts any set of dates back by one year; it works correctly — the user's expectation is wrong
options.explanation: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR returns the same set of dates shifted back exactly one year. For Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2024, it returns Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2023. This is mathematically correct. If the user expects something different (e.g., full year or specific business period), SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR is working as designed. The 'incorrect value' reflects the user's expectation, not a bug.

questions.reference: [SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR function (DAX) - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/sameperiodlastyear-function-dax)

questions.stem: Which Power BI storage mode allows data to be cached in the Power BI service for fast query performance while also allowing live queries to the source for the most recent data?

options.text: DirectQuery mode
options.explanation: DirectQuery always queries the source live and never caches data locally; there is no cached copy.

options.text: Dual (Mixed) mode
options.explanation: Dual mode (also called Mixed or Dual storage mode) allows a table to behave as either Import or DirectQuery depending on the query context. When used with dimension tables alongside DirectQuery fact tables, the engine queries cached data for dimensions (fast) and live data for facts (current).

options.text: Live connection to Analysis Services
options.explanation: A live connection to Analysis Services queries the remote model on every request; it does not cache data in the Power BI service.

options.text: Import mode
options.explanation: Import mode caches data locally but never queries the source live; data is only updated during scheduled or manual refreshes.

questions.reference: [Table Storage Mode in Power BI Semantic Models - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/transform-model/desktop-storage-mode)

questions.stem: You must unpivot a table where months are column headers (Jan, Feb, Mar...) and product names are row headers. The result should have columns: Product, Month, Sales. Which Power Query transformation achieves this most robustly as new month columns are added?

options.text: Select the Product column, then choose 'Unpivot Other Columns'
options.explanation: 'Unpivot Other Columns' (selecting the Product column and choosing Unpivot Other Columns) unpivots everything except the selected column. This approach is more robust than selecting individual month columns because it automatically includes any new month columns added to the source data without requiring query changes.

options.text: Select all month columns individually, then choose 'Unpivot Columns'
options.explanation: Explicitly selecting all month columns and using 'Unpivot Columns' requires manually adding each new month column to the selection when the source data grows; it is less maintainable than Unpivot Other Columns.

options.text: Use 'Transpose' followed by 'Use First Row as Headers'
options.explanation: Transpose followed by 'Use First Row as Headers' would produce a transposed table, not an unpivoted one with Product, Month, Sales columns.

options.text: Use 'Group By' on the Product column and aggregate month values
options.explanation: 'Group By' aggregates rows; it does not restructure columns into rows the way Unpivot does.

questions.reference: [Unpivot columns - Power Query](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-query/unpivot-column)

questions.stem: You want to create a date dimension table in Power BI that covers the range of dates found in your fact table's [Order Date] column automatically. Which approach generates this table without hardcoding date ranges?

options.text: Create a calculated table using CALENDARAUTO() in DAX
options.explanation: CALENDARAUTO() scans all date columns in the data model and automatically generates a contiguous date table from the earliest to the latest date found, with a one-row-per-day granularity. This ensures the date table always covers the fact table's date range as data grows.

options.text: Import a CSV with predefined dates from a static file
options.explanation: Importing a static CSV requires manual updates whenever the date range grows beyond the predefined bounds.

options.text: Mark the built-in auto date/time table as the date table
options.explanation: The built-in auto date/time table creates hidden per-column date hierarchies; it is not a proper date dimension table and is generally not recommended for complex models.

options.text: Use a Power Query M function with List.Dates and hardcoded start/end dates
options.explanation: List.Dates with hardcoded start/end dates is also static and requires manual updates when the date range changes.

questions.reference: [CALENDARAUTO function (DAX) - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/calendarauto-function-dax)

questions.stem: You have a single Sales table and need to create measures for 'Sales to New Customers' and 'Sales to Returning Customers'. A new customer is one whose first purchase was in the current filter context. Which DAX technique is most appropriate?

options.text: Create a customer segmentation calculated table and relate it to Sales
options.explanation: A customer segmentation calculated table computes segments at data load time and does not respond dynamically to the filter context when users change time periods.

options.text: Use two separate calculated columns: IsNewCustomer and IsReturningCustomer, then SUM them
options.explanation: Calculated columns compute row-by-row at load time and cannot respond dynamically to the current filter context; they would classify customers based on their global history, not the current period.

options.text: Use the built-in New vs Returning customer quick measure
options.explanation: Power BI Quick Measures may not be available for all versions and may not exactly match the required definition of 'new' customer based on the first purchase in the current filter context.

options.text: Use CALCULATE with FILTER and MINX to find customers whose minimum order date equals a date within the current period
options.explanation: The DAX pattern for new customer sales uses CALCULATE to evaluate sales for customers whose earliest purchase (MINX or CALCULATE(MIN(Sales[Date]), ALLEXCEPT(Sales, Sales[CustomerID]))) falls within the current filter context period. This is a FILTER-over-VALUES pattern that responds dynamically to the filter context.

questions.reference: [CALCULATE function (DAX)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/calculate-function-dax)

questions.stem: You have two queries: 'Orders' and 'Returns'. You must combine them to see all orders with a flag indicating whether each was returned. Some orders have no corresponding return record. Which Power Query merge join type should you use?

options.text: Inner join
options.explanation: An Inner join excludes orders that have no matching return record, meaning non-returned orders would be lost from the result.

options.text: Right anti join
options.explanation: A Right anti join returns only rows from the right table (Returns) that have no match in Orders — the opposite of what is needed.

options.text: Full outer join
options.explanation: A Full outer join also includes return records that have no matching order, adding unnecessary rows to the result.

options.text: Left outer join
options.explanation: A Left outer join keeps all rows from the left table (Orders) and brings in matching rows from the right table (Returns). Orders with no return match get null values in the Returns columns, which can be used to flag unmatched orders.

questions.reference: [Merge Queries Overview - Power Query](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-query/merge-queries-overview)

questions.stem: A CALCULATE expression uses REMOVEFILTERS(DimDate[Year]) as a filter modifier. What does REMOVEFILTERS do in this context compared to ALL(DimDate[Year])?

options.text: REMOVEFILTERS cannot be used with a column argument; it only accepts table arguments
options.explanation: REMOVEFILTERS accepts both table and column arguments, just like ALL.

options.text: REMOVEFILTERS and ALL(DimDate[Year]) are functionally identical as filter modifiers in CALCULATE
options.explanation: is partially correct in describing the outcome but 'functionally identical' is less precise than saying REMOVEFILTERS is a semantic alias — option D captures both the equivalence and the reason.

options.text: REMOVEFILTERS removes only external filters; ALL removes all filters including those from relationships
options.explanation: Both REMOVEFILTERS and ALL remove all filters on the specified column or table when used as CALCULATE modifiers; neither distinguishes between external and relationship-based filters.

options.text: REMOVEFILTERS is a newer alias for ALL when used as a CALCULATE modifier; they are semantically equivalent in this context
options.explanation: REMOVEFILTERS was introduced as a semantic alias for ALL when used as a CALCULATE filter modifier. REMOVEFILTERS(DimDate[Year]) and ALL(DimDate[Year]) in CALCULATE produce identical results: both remove any active filter on DimDate[Year] from the filter context. REMOVEFILTERS was introduced to make code intent clearer (removing filters vs. returning all values).

questions.reference: [REMOVEFILTERS function (DAX) - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/removefilters-function-dax)

questions.stem: A dataset refreshes from an on-premises SQL Server database. The refresh fails with 'Unable to connect to the data source'. The on-premises data gateway is installed and running. Which action should you check first?

options.text: Reinstall Power BI Desktop to clear gateway credentials
options.explanation: Tableau Desktop credentials are not involved in gateway-based refreshes; gateway credentials are managed in the Power BI service data source settings, not in Power BI Desktop.

options.text: Upgrade to a Premium capacity to enable on-premises refresh
options.explanation: Premium capacity is not required for on-premises data gateway refresh; it works with Pro licenses.

options.text: Verify that the gateway machine has outbound access to the Power BI service endpoints on port 443
options.explanation: The on-premises data gateway communicates with Power BI service via outbound HTTPS (port 443) and optionally Azure Service Bus (port 5671/5672). If the gateway is running but refresh fails, the most common cause is a firewall blocking outbound connectivity from the gateway machine to Power BI service endpoints.

options.text: Switch the dataset from Import to DirectQuery mode
options.explanation: Switching to DirectQuery mode changes the data architecture entirely and would still require the gateway to connect to the on-premises database.

questions.reference: [Troubleshoot the on-premises data gateway](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/data-integration/gateway/service-gateway-tshoot)

questions.stem: You have a Power BI report in a Premium workspace. You want to give external users from a partner organization read-only access to the report without creating Microsoft 365 accounts for them. What feature enables this?

options.text: Publish to web (public URL)
options.explanation: Publish to web creates a publicly accessible, unauthenticated URL — not appropriate for partner access with access control.

options.text: Export to PDF and email
options.explanation: Exporting to PDF provides a static snapshot; it does not allow interactive access or controlled sharing with authentication.

options.text: Power BI guest access via Azure AD B2B
options.explanation: Azure AD B2B (Business-to-Business) allows external users to access Power BI content using their own organizational identity. Administrators invite external users via Azure AD, and they access the report with read permissions without needing a Microsoft 365 account in your tenant.

options.text: Embed the report in a SharePoint Online page
options.explanation: Embedding the report in SharePoint Online requires the viewers to have Microsoft 365 accounts, which is exactly what the scenario is trying to avoid.

questions.reference: [Distribute Power BI content to external guest users](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/enterprise/powerbi/service-admin-entra-b2b)

questions.stem: Your organization wants to automate Power BI dataset refresh using the REST API from an Azure Data Factory pipeline after the ETL loads data. Which authentication method is recommended for service-to-service API calls?

options.text: API key generated in Power BI tenant settings
options.explanation: Power BI does not generate API keys in tenant settings; the correct mechanism is service principals with OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow.

options.text: Master user account with Power BI Pro license using username/password OAuth flow
options.explanation: Master user accounts with username/password OAuth flow are fragile, a security risk, and are not the recommended approach for automated pipelines.

options.text: User account credentials stored in Azure Key Vault
options.explanation: Storing user account credentials in Key Vault still uses a user account (with its associated license and MFA requirements), which is less robust than service principal authentication.

options.text: Service principal with the dataset's workspace role assignment and Power BI Admin API permission
options.explanation: Service principals (Azure AD application registrations) are the recommended authentication method for service-to-service Power BI REST API automation. They do not require a licensed user account, support certificate or secret authentication, and permissions are granted via workspace roles (Member or Admin). Service principals must be enabled in Power BI tenant settings.

questions.reference: [Embed Power BI content in an embedded analytics application with service principal and an application secret - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/developer/embedded/embed-service-principal)

questions.stem: You must display sales by country on a map visual. Countries are stored as two-letter ISO codes (e.g., 'US', 'GB'). The map shows incorrect locations for some countries. Which action should you do?

options.text: Replace ISO codes with full country names
options.explanation: Replacing ISO codes with full country names is unnecessary — Power BI's geocoding engine supports ISO country codes natively once the data category is set correctly.

options.text: Use a filled map instead of a bubble map
options.explanation: Switching between filled map and bubble map changes the visual type but does not affect geocoding accuracy; the underlying geocoding issue remains.

options.text: Set the Data Category of the country column to 'Country or Region' in the column properties
options.explanation: Power BI uses data category metadata to interpret geographic data correctly. Setting the Data Category to 'Country or Region' (via Column Tools tab in Power BI Desktop) helps the map visual and Bing geocoder understand that the column contains country identifiers. Without this, Power BI may misinterpret 'US' as a US state abbreviation or city name.

options.text: Enable the 'Use Microsoft Bing geocoding' option in report settings
options.explanation: Microsoft Bing geocoding is used by default for map visuals; enabling it explicitly is not an available report setting that would fix geocoding.

questions.reference: [Data categorization in Power BI Desktop - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/transform-model/desktop-data-categorization)

questions.stem: A report uses bookmarks to switch between two chart types (bar and line) showing the same data. When the user selects a region from a slicer and then switches bookmarks, the slicer selection is lost. Why?

options.text: Bookmarks capture visual state including data filters by default; the slicer state was captured at bookmark creation time
options.explanation: Power BI bookmarks capture the state of the report at the time they are created, including data filters (slicer selections), display state (which visuals are visible), and spotlight. When a bookmark is applied, it restores the exact state it captured — overwriting the user's current slicer selection. To preserve user slicer state when applying bookmarks for chart-type toggling, uncheck 'Data' in the bookmark's properties so only the visual visibility is changed.

options.text: Slicers are not supported in bookmark-based navigation
options.explanation: Slicers are fully supported in bookmark-based navigation; the issue is that bookmarks capture slicer state by default.

options.text: Cross-page filtering is not preserved by bookmarks
options.explanation: This scenario involves bookmarks on the same page, not cross-page filtering.

options.text: The bookmark was created without capturing slicer state; enable 'Data' in bookmark properties
options.explanation: The problem is that the bookmark's 'Data' property IS capturing slicer state (overwriting the user's selection). The fix is to uncheck 'Data', not enable it.

questions.reference: [Create report bookmarks in Power BI - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/create-reports/desktop-bookmarks)

questions.stem: A Q&A visual is not recognizing the synonym 'revenue' for the measure named 'Total Sales'. How do you fix this?

options.text: Create a second measure named 'Revenue' that references [Total Sales]
options.explanation: Creating a duplicate measure adds confusion and maintenance overhead; synonyms are the purpose-built solution.

options.text: Rename the measure to 'Revenue'
options.explanation: Renaming the measure would break any existing report references and calculated field expressions that use [Total Sales].

options.text: Add 'revenue' as a synonym in the Q&A linguistic schema / Synonyms feature in the model
options.explanation: Power BI Q&A supports custom synonyms defined in the Synonyms pane (accessible via the model view or via the Q&A setup in the report). Adding 'revenue' as a synonym for the [Total Sales] measure allows Q&A to recognize user queries using either term.

options.text: Enable the Q&A natural language training feature in Power BI service settings
options.explanation: NLP is always enabled for Q&A visuals; there is no separate 'enable NLP' toggle in Power BI service settings that would fix this synonym issue.

questions.reference: [Teach Q&A to understand questions and terms in Power BI Q&A - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/natural-language/q-and-a-tooling-teach-q-and-a)

questions.stem: A data model contains a Sales fact table and a Customers dimension. You need a measure that calculates the average sales per customer, counting only customers who made at least one purchase in the filtered context. Which DAX expression is correct?

options.text: AVERAGE(Sales[Amount])
options.explanation: AVERAGE(Sales[Amount]) averages at the individual transaction row level, not at the customer level, giving average sales per transaction rather than per customer.

options.text: DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[CustomerID]))
options.explanation: DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[CustomerID])) calculates total sales divided by the count of distinct customers who appear in the Sales table within the filter context — i.e., only customers who made purchases.

options.text: DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), COUNTROWS(Customers))
options.explanation: COUNTROWS(Customers) would count all customers in the Customers dimension table regardless of whether they purchased in the filter context, which inflates the denominator.

options.text: AVERAGEX(Customers, SUM(Sales[Amount]))
options.explanation: AVERAGEX(Customers, SUM(Sales[Amount])) iterates over all customers in the Customers table (not just those with purchases) and averages their individual SUM([Sales]), which would return 0 or blank for non-purchasing customers and distort the average.

questions.reference: [DISTINCTCOUNT function (DAX) - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/distinctcount-function-dax)

questions.stem: A bar chart shows top 10 products by sales. You want to add a reference line showing the average sales across all products regardless of the top 10 filter. Which approach achieves this?

options.text: Add an analytics pane average line using 'Average' from the Analytics panel
options.explanation: The Analytics pane average line computes the average within the current visual filter context (top 10 products only), not across all products in the dataset.

options.text: Use a constant line from the Analytics pane with a hardcoded average value
options.explanation: A constant line with a hardcoded average value would not update automatically as the underlying data changes.

options.text: Create a measure: AVG All Products = CALCULATE(AVERAGE(Sales[Amount]), ALL(Products)) and add it to the visual
options.explanation: A DAX measure using CALCULATE with ALL(Products) removes the product filter context, so the average is computed across all products in the dataset regardless of the Top N filter applied to the visual. This measure can then be plotted as a separate measure series or added as a constant line with a dynamic value.

options.text: Enable the 'Show data labels' option on the bar chart to display averages
options.explanation: 'Show data labels' displays values for each bar; it does not add a reference line showing the all-products average.

questions.reference: [CALCULATE function (DAX) - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/calculate-function-dax)

questions.stem: You want to certify a dataset in your organization so that report authors know it is the trusted, authoritative source. Which Power BI feature enables this?

options.text: Set the dataset as 'Featured' in the workspace
options.explanation: 'Featured' content refers to pinning content to the Home page, not to a governance endorsement for data quality.

options.text: Apply the 'Certified' endorsement to the dataset
options.explanation: Power BI supports dataset endorsement with two levels: 'Promoted' (self-service, set by the dataset owner) and 'Certified' (set by administrators or designated certifiers after quality review). Certified datasets display a certification badge in the data hub and dataset search results.

options.text: Add a 'Certified' tag using workspace metadata settings
options.explanation: Tags in Power BI are free-form metadata for search/discovery; there is no 'Certified' tag mechanism — the certification is done through the formal endorsement feature.

options.text: Mark the dataset as 'Official' in Power BI Desktop
options.explanation: There is no 'Official' designation in Power BI Desktop; the endorsement feature (Promoted/Certified) is configured in the Power BI service.

questions.reference: [Endorse your content - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/collaborate-share/service-endorse-content)

questions.stem: A Power Query table has a column named [Sales Amount] that contains text values like '$1,234.56'. You must convert it to a decimal number. What is the correct sequence of transformations?

options.text: Add a custom column using Number.FromText([Sales Amount])
options.explanation: Number.FromText('[Sales Amount]') also fails when the string contains '$' and ',' characters; the string must be cleaned first.

options.text: Change the column data type directly to Decimal Number
options.explanation: Power Query cannot automatically parse '$1,234.56' as a Decimal Number; the currency symbol and thousands separator cause a type conversion error.

options.text: Use Text.ToNumber in a new custom column
options.explanation: Text.ToNumber does not exist as a Power Query M function; the correct function is Number.FromText, and even that requires a clean numeric string.

options.text: Use Replace Values to remove '$' and ',', then change data type to Decimal Number
options.explanation: Directly changing a text column with currency symbols and thousand separators to Decimal Number causes conversion errors. The correct approach is to first clean the text: Replace Values to remove '$', then Replace Values to remove ',', and finally change the data type to Decimal Number.

questions.reference: [Use Power Query to transform data - Power Query](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-query/power-query-ui)

questions.stem: A Power Query step transforms a column using a formula that references a column that was renamed in a later step. The query throws an error. What is the root cause?

options.text: Power Query does not support renaming columns that are referenced in formulas
options.explanation: Power Query does support renaming columns used in formulas; the issue is step ordering, not a feature restriction.

options.text: The formula references a column that was renamed, so it cannot find the original name
options.explanation: Power Query evaluates each step sequentially. If a rename step is inserted before an existing formula step that uses the old column name, the formula step can no longer find the column by its original name. The column name in the formula must match the name at that point in the step sequence.

options.text: Power Query formulas must use column index positions, not names
options.explanation: Power Query M formulas reference columns by name (e.g., [ColumnName]), not by index position.

options.text: Power Query steps are evaluated top-to-bottom; the column is renamed in a later step, so at the time of the formula step the original name is valid — renaming later causes downstream steps to fail
options.explanation: is a valid description but is less direct than B — the key point is that the formula cannot find the original name because a rename occurred before this step in the sequence.

questions.reference: [Use Power Query to transform data - Power Query](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-query/power-query-ui)

questions.stem: You must combine data from 12 monthly Excel files in a folder into a single table. Each file has the same schema. Which Power Query approach is most maintainable as new monthly files are added?

options.text: Use a Power Query loop to iterate over file names
options.explanation: Power Query has no loop construct; iteration over files is handled by the folder connector's combine function.

options.text: Manually import each file and append the 12 queries
options.explanation: Manually importing each file and appending queries requires adding a new query each month, which is not maintainable.

options.text: Create an ODBC connection to the folder
options.explanation: An ODBC connection to a folder is not a standard Power Query connector type for Excel files.

options.text: Use 'Get Data > Folder', combine files using the combine function, which automatically processes all files in the folder
options.explanation: The Power Query folder connector (Get Data > Folder) lists all files in a directory and applies a combine function to merge them. When new monthly files are added to the folder, refreshing the query automatically includes the new files — no query changes needed.

questions.reference: [Combine files overview - Power Query](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-query/combine-files-overview)

questions.stem: A measure [Running Total] = CALCULATE([Total Sales], FILTER(ALL(Dates[Date]), Dates[Date] <= MAX(Dates[Date]))) works correctly in a table sorted by date. However, in a matrix with months as columns, it shows the same value (grand total) in all cells. What is the issue?

options.text: The matrix column context does not generate a row context; use EARLIER() to fix this
options.explanation: EARLIER() is used for row-context-to-filter-context transitions in calculated columns, not for fixing matrix running total issues.

options.text: MAX(Dates[Date]) in a matrix column context returns the max date for that column's month; the FILTER should use LASTDATE(Dates[Date]) instead
options.explanation: The issue is not that MAX returns the wrong value for the month — the issue is that ALL removes the column filter entirely before MAX evaluates.

options.text: ALL(Dates[Date]) removes the matrix column filter, and MAX(Dates[Date]) within FILTER evaluates to the overall max date; use ALLEXCEPT or ensure the filter context from columns is preserved
options.explanation: ALL(Dates[Date]) removes all filters on Dates[Date], including the matrix column filter. MAX(Dates[Date]) then evaluates over all dates (returning the overall maximum), so every column shows the same grand total running sum. The fix is to use a column filter that does not remove the matrix context, or restructure as CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date])) for YTD, or use ALLSELECTED to preserve external filters.

options.text: Running totals are only supported in table and line chart visuals, not matrices
options.explanation: Running totals work in matrix visuals when the DAX measure is written correctly; the issue is in the measure logic, not a visual limitation.

questions.reference: [Use variables to improve your DAX formulas - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/best-practices/dax-variables)

questions.stem: You must implement row-level security (RLS) so that each regional manager can only see data for their own region. The mapping of managers to regions is in a separate table 'ManagerRegions'. What is the correct setup?

options.text: Use Object Level Security to hide the Region column from non-admin users
options.explanation: Object Level Security hides columns or tables from users but does not filter row data; managers could still see all regions if they accessed the data through another route.

options.text: Create an RLS role with a DAX filter: [Region] = USERPRINCIPALNAME()
options.explanation: Applying USERPRINCIPALNAME() directly on [Region] only works if regions are named with email addresses, which is not the case here.

options.text: Create a calculated column in the fact table using USERPRINCIPALNAME() and filter on it
options.explanation: USERPRINCIPALNAME() returns a value at query time based on the current user; it cannot be used in a calculated column, which is computed at data load time without a user context.

options.text: Create an RLS role that filters the ManagerRegions table: [ManagerEmail] = USERPRINCIPALNAME(), ensure ManagerRegions relates to the Region dimension
options.explanation: Dynamic RLS using USERPRINCIPALNAME() should be applied on the ManagerRegions mapping table, which relates to the Region dimension. The DAX filter [ManagerEmail] = USERPRINCIPALNAME() restricts the ManagerRegions table to the logged-in user's row(s), which cascades through the relationship to filter the Region dimension and ultimately the fact table.

questions.reference: [Row-level security (RLS) with Power BI - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/enterprise/service-admin-rls)

questions.stem: Which Power BI visual type is best for showing the distribution of a continuous variable (such as order amounts) and identifying skewness or outliers?

options.text: Histogram or box plot visual
options.explanation: A histogram shows the frequency distribution of a continuous variable across buckets, making it easy to identify the shape, central tendency, and spread. A box plot shows quartiles, median, and outliers directly. Both are purpose-built for distribution analysis.

options.text: Clustered bar chart
options.explanation: A clustered bar chart compares discrete categorical values; it does not show the distribution or spread of a continuous variable.

options.text: Donut chart
options.explanation: A donut chart shows part-to-whole proportions for a small number of discrete categories, not a continuous distribution.

options.text: Funnel chart
options.explanation: A funnel chart shows sequential stages in a process; it is not designed for distribution analysis or outlier identification.

questions.reference: [Overview of visualizations in Power BI - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/visuals/power-bi-visualization-types-for-reports-and-q-and-a)

questions.stem: You want to highlight values in a table visual where sales are below target. Sales above or equal to target should be green; below target should be red. Which feature should you use?

options.text: Create a conditional formatting rule using background color with rules-based formatting on the sales column
options.explanation: Power BI's conditional formatting for table and matrix visuals allows rules-based background or font color formatting. You configure rules like 'If value < [Target] then Red else Green'. This is available in the Format pane under 'Conditional formatting' and dynamically responds to filter context.

options.text: Create a calculated column returning 'Green' or 'Red' and add it to the table
options.explanation: A calculated column returning 'Green' or 'Red' computes at data load time (not filter context) and adds a text column to the table rather than coloring cells.

options.text: Add a KPI visual next to the table
options.explanation: A KPI visual is a separate visual type for comparing a measure against a target; it does not color individual cells within a table visual.

options.text: Use a data bar with a positive/negative threshold
options.explanation: Data bars show relative values as horizontal bar fills within a cell; they do not apply red/green threshold-based background color formatting.

questions.reference: [Apply Conditional Table Formatting in Power BI - Power BI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/create-reports/desktop-conditional-table-formatting)

questions.stem: You must calculate year-to-date (YTD) total sales in a DAX measure. The fiscal year ends on June 30. Which DAX function correctly handles the non-calendar fiscal year end date?

options.text: CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date], "06/30"))
options.explanation: CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date], "06/30")) uses DATESYTD with the optional year_end_date parameter set to '06/30', which instructs DAX to treat June 30 as the last day of the fiscal year. This produces a YTD calculation that resets on July 1 each year.

options.text: TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date], "06-30")
options.explanation: is also technically correct (TOTALYTD with "06-30" uses the same underlying logic), but option A is more explicit in how it constructs the fiscal YTD and is the most commonly recommended form. When two answers appear correct, the question asks for the one that 'correctly handles' the requirement — A is the clearest expression.

options.text: CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date]))
options.explanation: DATESYTD without a year_end_date parameter defaults to December 31, so this would calculate a calendar YTD rather than a fiscal YTD ending June 30.

options.text: TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date])
options.explanation: TOTALYTD without a year_end_date parameter also defaults to December 31, not June 30.

questions.reference: [DATESYTD function (DAX) - DAX](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dax/datesytd-function-dax)
